independence_day.intro: Brazil's Independence Day on 7 September marks the 1822 break from Portugal proclaimed by Prince Pedro.
independence_day.history: On 7 September 1822, by the Ipiranga brook in São Paulo, Prince Regent Pedro declared Brazil independent from Portugal with the cry ‚Independence or death!‘, becoming Emperor Pedro I. Portugal recognised independence in 1825, ending three centuries of colonial rule.
independence_day.traditions: A grand civic-military parade is held in Brasília before the President, with the air force’s Esquadrilha da Fumaça trailing green and yellow smoke. Towns and schools across Brazil hold their own parades and flag ceremonies.
funFacts: The proclamation is known as the ‚Grito do Ipiranga‘.
Pedro I declared independence and became emperor at just 23.
The Esquadrilha da Fumaça paints the sky green and yellow.

carnival.intro: Brazil's Carnival is the country's most famous festival, a pre-Lenten explosion of samba, parades and street parties.
carnival.history: Carnival arrived with Portuguese colonists and merged with African rhythms brought by enslaved people, giving birth to samba. The first samba schools formed in Rio in the late 1920s, and the Sambadrome parades grew into a global spectacle.
carnival.traditions: In Rio the samba schools compete in the Sambadrome with elaborate floats and costumes, while Salvador fills with electric ‚trios elétricos‘ and Olinda with giant puppets. Free street ‚blocos‘ draw millions of dancers for days.
funFacts: Rio's Sambadrome was designed by architect Oscar Niemeyer.
Carnival dates shift each year as it precedes Lent.
Salvador's street carnival is among the largest in the world.
